cards.front: What's the purpose of a management IP on a Layer 2 Cisco switch, and where is it configured?
cards.back: L2 switches don't route â€” they need an IP only so admins can SSH/Telnet/HTTP in remotely. Configured on a special interface called the management VLAN's SVI: `interface vlan 1` (or whichever mgmt VLAN), then `ip address X Y` and `no shutdown`. Plus `ip default-gateway` for off-subnet access.

cards.front: Configure SSH on a Cisco switch â€” list the required steps.
cards.back: (1) Set hostname (not 'Switch') and `ip domain-name <name>` (RSA key needs both). (2) `crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048` (or 1024). (3) Local user with `username X secret Y` or AAA. (4) `line vty 0 15` â†’ `login local` (or `login authentication`) â†’ `transport input ssh` (or `ssh telnet`). (5) Ideally `ip ssh version 2`.

cards.front: What are the line types on a switch and what does each represent?
cards.back: `line console 0` â€” physical RJ-45/USB console port. `line vty 0 15` â€” 16 virtual TTYs for Telnet/SSH (numbered 0-15). `line aux 0` â€” auxiliary port (modem on routers, rarely on switches). Each line can have its own login method, password, transport.

cards.front: What's the difference between `enable password` and `enable secret`?
cards.back: `enable password` stores the password in clear text (Type 0) or weak Type 7 reversible encryption. `enable secret` hashes with MD5 (Type 5) or stronger (Type 8 PBKDF2, Type 9 scrypt). If both are set, `enable secret` always wins. Modern best practice: only use `enable secret`.

cards.front: What does `service password-encryption` do, and what type results?
cards.back: Applies Cisco's Type 7 reversible 'encryption' (really obfuscation) to existing and future plain-text passwords in the config (e.g., `enable password`, line passwords). Easily reversible â€” DOES NOT secure passwords; use `enable secret` and `username â€¦ secret` instead.

cards.front: What's the purpose of `banner motd`, `banner login`, and `banner exec`?
cards.back: MOTD (message of the day): displayed before login prompt, all sessions. Login: displayed after MOTD, before username prompt. Exec: displayed AFTER successful authentication. Syntax: `banner motd #...text...#` where `#` is a delimiter (any character not in the message).

cards.front: What does `no shutdown` do, and why is it needed on switch SVIs but not access ports?
cards.back: Brings an interface administratively up. SVIs and router interfaces default to `shutdown` (administratively down) and require `no shutdown`. Switch access ports default to administratively UP, so they don't need it.

cards.front: How do you save the running-config to NVRAM in two equivalent commands?
cards.back: `copy running-config startup-config` (full form) or `write memory` (short form, often abbreviated `wr`). Both write the in-RAM config to NVRAM as the startup-config.
